coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by a microwave assisted hydrothermal method which enhanced fluorescence. When combined with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), N-CQDs exhibited a “turn-off/on” fluorescence response in the presence of the antibiotic meropenem. Remarkably, the N-CQDs/AuNPs enabled both fluorescence and colorimetric detection. Colorimetric sensing relied on a visible color change of the AuNPs from red to blue, triggered by the addition of meropenem, while fluorescence sensing was based on fluorescence recovery. Quantification was achieved by monitoring the absorbance ratio changes at 670 nm and 530 nm for colorimetry and fluorescence intensity recovery for fluorometry, both as functions of meropenem concentration. This dual-mode detection system presents a cost-effective, and environmentally friendly platform for both straightforward visual detection and ultrahigh sensitive (LOD 1 ppb) quantification of meropenem across a wide range of concentrations from 8 to 850 nM. The fluorescence-based method was successfully applied to analyze meropenem in pharmaceutical samples and human plasma, showing high reliability when cross-validated against the conventional LC-MS/MS technique.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/AuNPs composite: (a) Absorption spectra, (b, c) EDS, (d) TEM image (scale bar 50 nm), (e) XRD, (f) FT-IR spectra and (g-k) XPS spectra.
Schematic representation of using N-CQDs/AuNPs to detect meropenem.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs, N-CQDs with meropenem 100 nM, N-CQDs/AuNPs and N-CQDs/AuNPs with meropenem 100 nM; (b) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs/AuNPs at different meropenem concentrations from 4 − 500 nM, with Cu2+ 0.1 mM; (c) UV–Vis spectra of N-CQDs/AuNPs with meropenem at different concentrations from 500 to 5000 nM, with Cu2+ 0.1 mM.

(a) Effect of pH solution, (b) ζ of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/AuNPs, N-CQDs/AuNPs/meropenem at different pHs, (c) Speciation of meropenem at different pHs, (d) Effect of incubation time and (e) Effect of typical electrolyte salts on fluorescence recovery.
Calibration curve of meropenem determination using (a) colorimetric and (b) flourescence method. (c) Selectivity of fluorescence method using N-CQDs/AuNPs composite.
Figures of merit of meropenem detection using CQDs/AuNPs and N-CQDs/AuNPs.
Comparison of limit of detection (LOD) values from previous studies on meropenem detection.
Concentrations of meropenem in several pharmaceutical samples determined by UV–Vis and LC-MS/MS methods.
Meropenem determination in spiked human plasma.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) synthesized by a microwave assisted hydrothermal method in combination with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as sensitive dual sensor for antibiotic meropenem detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by a microwave assisted hydrothermal method which enhanced fluorescence. When combined with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), N-CQDs exhibited a “turn-off/on” fluorescence response in the presence of the antibiotic meropenem. Remarkably, the N-CQDs/AuNPs enabled both fluorescence and colorimetric detection. Colorimetric sensing relied on a visible color change of the AuNPs from red to blue, triggered by the addition of meropenem, while fluorescence sensing was based on fluorescence recovery. Quantification was achieved by monitoring the absorbance ratio changes at 670 nm and 530 nm for colorimetry and fluorescence intensity recovery for fluorometry, both as functions of meropenem concentration. This dual-mode detection system presents a cost-effective, and environmentally friendly platform for both straightforward visual detection and ultrahigh sensitive (LOD 1 ppb) quantification of meropenem across a wide range of concentrations from 8 to 850 nM. The fluorescence-based method was successfully applied to analyze meropenem in pharmaceutical samples and human plasma, showing high reliability when cross-validated against the conventional LC-MS/MS technique.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Capillary electrophoresis instrument with contactless conductivity detection

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Meropenem, a carbapenem β-lactam antibiotic, exhibits broad-spectrum activity against aerobic and anaerobic Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [1,2]. It is commonly administered to treat severe infections, including meningitis, pneumonia, intra-abdominal infections, and sepsis, primarily due to its bactericidal effect on bacterial cell wall synthesis. Notably, meropenem remains the only carbapenem approved as an alternative therapy for meningitis and is considered safe during pregnancy. Its strong stability against β-lactamases contributes to relatively low resistance rates, positioning meropenem as a last-resort antibiotic in intensive care units [3]. Nevertheless, balancing the dose is crucial: insufficient dosing fosters resistance, whereas overdosing poses a risk of adverse effects. Patients with renal impairment, frequent in critical care, face an elevated risk of meropenem accumulation, underscoring the importance of therapeutic drug level monitoring [4].

Several analytical methods, including square wave voltammetry (SWV) [1], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [2,5], and capillary electrophoresis [6,7], have been employed to measure meropenem levels. However, these techniques often require complex instrumentation, rigorous protocols, and large amounts of organic solvents, highlighting the need for simpler, more cost-effective, and environmentally friendly alternatives. Fluorescence analysis has emerged as a promising contender owing to its speed, sensitivity, and compatibility with portable devices [8,9]. By selecting appropriate fluorescent probes, rapid, on-site, and highly sensitive detection can be achieved [8,10]. Recent reports also emphasize fluorescence as a viable approach for antibiotic detection, reflecting its growing significance in analytical applications.

Among emerging fluorescent materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial under 10 nm, have gained considerable attention [11 –14]. CQDs boast strong photoluminescence, excellent water solubility, low toxicity, and robust surface chemical tunability, making them ideal for sensors, bio-imaging, drug delivery, and photocatalysis. They have also shown efficacy as fluorescent probes for detecting contaminants in environmental, food, and biological matrices [15 –20]. Doping CQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), phosphorus (P), and chlorine (Cl) further refines their optical and electronic characteristics [21,22]. In particular, nitrogen doping enhances luminescence and introduces amine groups (–NH2) that improve water solubility and facilitate stronger interactions with diverse functional groups [23 –27].

Common synthesis routes for N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs), including electrochemical methods, pyrolysis, and hydrolysis [27 –30], can be energy-intensive, time-consuming, or reliant on toxic solvents. To overcome these drawbacks, we adopted a green, microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique using a standard household microwave. This approach notably shortens reaction times from hours to mere minutes and reduces energy consumption compared to conventional pyrolysis.

Once synthesized, CQDs frequently serve in fluorescence-quenching assays to detect various inorganic and organic targets. When direct interactions are limited, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) can mediate detection due to their surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and strong visible-light absorption [31 –33]. AuNPs quench N-CQDs fluorescence (“off” state), and analyte binding restores it (“on” state), enabling sensitive and selective antibiotic detection [34 –36]. Typically, AuNPs are formed by reducing chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) in the presence of a stabilizer [37 –39]. However, the carboxylic groups on N-CQDs also enable them to act as both reducing and stabilizing agents during AuNP synthesis.

In this study, we propose a novel strategy for meropenem detection and quantification at parts-per-billion (ppb) levels by combining microwave-synthesized N-CQDs with AuNPs in a single-step process. N-CQDs function as both reductants and stabilizers, and the transition between “off” and “on” fluorescence states allows accurate quantification of meropenem across 8–500 nM. Demonstrating excellent performance in both pharmaceutical products and human plasma, this approach circumvents the toxicity issues of conventional quantum dots (e.g., CdSe, CdTe) [40,41]. Moreover, the N-CQDs/AuNPs platform offers a colorimetric mode based on shifts in localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) wavelength, visible as a color change from red to blue. By measuring absorbance ratios at 530 nm and 670 nm, the method enables quantification over 80–850 nM, providing a dual-mode detection system. Overall, this cost-effective, eco-friendly platform supports both straightforward visual screening and sensitive detection of meropenem across a broad concentration range.

All solutions were prepared using ultrapure deionized (DI) water obtained from a Milli-Q purification system (Millipore, Darmstadt, Germany). Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), tetrachloroauric (III) acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), urea (CH4N2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), were procured from Merck (Germany) and Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Antibiotics including meropenem, ertapenem, penicillin V, cefalexin, and amoxicillin were provided by the National Institute of Drug Quality Control (Vietnam) and purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) or TRC (Canada).

UV–Vis spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-1650PC spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorometric measurements were made using a Hitachi F-4700 spectrofluorometer (Japan). Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD) and Shimadzu Affinity 1S Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy were used to analyze the crystal phase structure and functional groups on the samples, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) in conjunction with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were performed using a JEOL JEM 1010 and JSM-IT200 systems, respectively, while X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a K-Alpha Plus instrument (Thermo Scientific). The surface charge was determined by a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, England).

Samples were cross-checked by liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), using an Exion LC 20AD (Sciex, USA) coupled to an AB SCIEX Triple Quad 6500+ (Sciex, USA), employing an Agilent eclipses plus C18, 3.5 µm, 2.1 × 150 mm column and HCOOH 0.1 % mobile phase (LOD ∼ 3 ppb).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs were synthesized using a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, following a procedure previously reported by our group [2]. Briefly, 0.77 g of citric acid (4 mmol), 0.013 g of urea (0.2 mmol), and 5 mL of deionized water were added to a 100 mL beaker and stirred until fully dissolved. The mixture was then irradiated in a household microwave at a power of 600 W for 5 min, during which the solution turned orange, indicating the formation of N-CQDs. To further process the synthesized N-CQDs, 10 mL of deionized water was added to the reaction product, and the mixture was subjected to ultra-sonication for 10 min to ensure complete dissolution. The solution was subsequently centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min to remove any large particles, followed by filtration through a 0.22 μm microfilter. Finally, the filtered solution was lyophilized to obtain a brown-blackish solid, which was stored for further use.

N-CQDs/AuNPs composite was synthesized by utilizing N-CQDs as both reducing and stabilizing agents to convert HAuCl4 into AuNPs. In this process, the N-CQDs act similarly to citric acid in the conventional Turkevich method, reducing Au (III) to Au (0) while simultaneously binding to the formed AuNPs to ensure stabilization [39]. Notably, this reduction process does not require boiling, simplifying the synthesis. Specifically, 375 μL of 10 mM HAuCl4 solution was added to 5 mL of the prepared N-CQDs solution. The mixture was left to react at room temperature for approximately 50 min, allowing the reduction to complete. The final solution turned wine red, confirming the successful formation of AuNPs within the composite.

The pH of 1.0 mL of N-CQDs/AuNPs solution was first adjusted to 2. Then, 20 µL of 0.01 M CuSO4 was added to the solution. Subsequently, a meropenem solution was introduced, and the mixture was diluted to a final volume of 2.0 mL with deionized water. The reaction mixture was allowed to stand at room temperature for 10 min, during which the solution's color changed from wine red to dark blue, indicating interaction between the components.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength (λex) of 360 nm and an emission wavelength (λem) of 445 nm. To quantify meropenem, the fluorescence recovery (F-F0) at 445 nm was calculated, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of meropenem, respectively. This fluorescence recovery serves as the analytical signal for determining meropenem concentration using fluorescence.

The analytical procedure follows the same route as mentioned in Section 2.5. But instead of fluorescence spectra, absorption spectra were obtained in the wavelength range from 400 − 800 nm. The absorbance ratio between 660 nm and 530 nm (A660/A530) served as the basis for quantifying meropenem by colorimetry.

Pharmaceutical samples: A pharmaceutical sample was dissolved in water, filtered, and diluted to 25 mL in a calibration flask. Then, 300 µL of the diluted solution was mixed with the N-CQDs/AuNPs solution and analyzed as described in Section 2.5.

Serum samples spiked: A 1 mL plasma sample spiked with meropenem at varying concentrations was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was transferred to a 15 mL falcon tube, adjusted to pH 5 using 0.1 M HCl, and extracted with 3 mL of chloroform for 15 min. After a second centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, the chloroform layer was collected, evaporated to dryness under nitrogen, and redissolved in 2 mL of deionized water. Next, 200 µL of the diluted sample solution was mixed with 1 mL of N-CQDs/AuNPs solution, followed by the addition of 20 µL of 0.01 M CuSO4 and pH adjustment to 2.0. The mixture was diluted to 2 mL and analyzed as outlined in Section 2.5.

When N-CQDs were added to the HAuCl4 solution, N-CQDs reduced Au3+ to Au (0) facilitating the formation of AuNPs. Simultaneously, the N-CQDs adsorbed onto the surface of the newly formed AuNPs, creating a stabilizing protective shell. This shell prevented the AuNPs from aggregating or flocculating, ensuring their stability and uniform dispersion in the solution. The solution's color gradually changed from light yellow to dark red, indicating the formation of AuNPs. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the N-CQDs/AuNPs solution (Fig. 1 a) displayed a prominent absorption peak at 530 nm, characteristic of AuNPs with sizes ranging from 20 to 30 nm. Additionally, a typical absorption peak at 320 nm was observed, corresponding to the π- π* transitions of –COOH and –NH2 functional groups on the surface of the N-CQDs [42]. Compared to the UV–Vis spectrum of the N-CQDs solution, the absorption maximum at 320 nm exhibited a slight red shift and a decrease in intensity. This shift and reduction likely result from the interaction of the –COOH and –NH2 groups on the N-CQDs with the surface of the newly formed AuNPs. This interaction indicates the successful integration of N-CQDs with AuNPs.

When excited at 360 nm, the synthesized N-CQDs exhibited intense green fluorescence with a quantum yield (QY) of 36.6 %. Interestingly, while nitrogen doping enhances the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs, the extent of nitrogen doping significantly impacts their reducing ability. As the ratio of urea to citric acid increases, the reducing capability of N-CQDs diminishes. At a specific ratio of 0.015 g urea to 0.077 g citric acid, the N-CQDs lose their ability to reduce auric salts to elemental gold entirely. This observation highlights the trade-off between fluorescence enhancement and reducing efficiency in N-CQDs synthesis, governed by the nitrogen doping level.

The chemical composition of the material was evaluated by EDS measurement. The results showed that the N-CQDs material contained 3 elements C, N and O with carbon (64 %), oxygen (29 %) and nitrogen (5 %), confirming the successful introduction of heteroatom N into N-CQDs (Fig. 1b). With added Au, N-CQDs/AuNPs composite material contained carbon (60 %), oxygen (23 %) and nitrogen (12 %) and Au (6 %) (Fig. 1c).

The TEM image (Fig. 1d) reveals two distinct types of particles: larger spherical particles, averaging around 20 nm in size, likely representing AuNPs, and smaller particles, approximately 5 nm in size, surrounding the larger ones, which can be attributed to N-CQDs. The XRD pattern of the N-CQDs/AuNPs composite (Fig. 1e) showed a broad diffraction halo at approximately 2θ = 22°, which is typical of the amorphous structure of N-CQDs. In addition, distinct diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ = 38°, 44° and 66°, corresponding to the (111), (200), and (220) crystal planes of metallic gold (Au), respectively. These peaks confirm the presence of AuNPs in the N-CQDs/AuNPs composite, further supporting the successful synthesis of the hybrid material.

To better understand the formation of functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs, as well as the bonding between N-CQDs/AuNPs and meropenem, FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/AuNPs without and with meropenem addtion were recorded. The FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. 1f) showed vibrations at 3337 cm−1, 2922 cm−1, 1237 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the O-H group, N-H group and C-N bond (aromatic amine), respectively, proving that N-CQDs were successfully synthesized from citric acid and urea using the microwave method. The FTIR spectra of CQDs/AuNPs in Fig. 1f displayed a notable change, particularly in the absorption peaks at 1624 cm−1 and 1034 cm−1. The disappearance of the well-defined C=O peak suggests disruption of the carboxylic acid functional group, likely due to CQDs adsorbing onto the AuNPs surface. Additionally, the absorption peak at 1381 cm−1 significantly decreases in intensity in the presence of AuNPs [23]. Upon the addition of meropenem, the intensity in the region from 3444 − 3600 cm−1 increases significantly, indicating the presence of the O–H group and N–H bond [43,44]. Further evidence of carboxylic acid groups is seen in two characteristic peaks at 1764 cm−1 and 1710 cm−1. The interaction of meropenem (containing an amine group) with the surface results in the formation of an amide, evidenced by two symmetrical peaks at 1764 cm−1 (amide 2, adjacent to the carbonyl group) and an asymmetric peak at 1710 cm−1 (amide 1, representing the C=O group).

The surface chemical composition of the N-CQDs/AuNPs composite was further clarified by XPS measurement. The resulting spectrum (Fig. 1g) displays distinct peaks for N, C, O, and Au, confirming the purity of the composite without detectable impurities. In the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1h), three major peaks are observed at 399.3, 400.0, and 401.3 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, N–H, and amide C–N groups, respectively [23,42]. The narrow scan C 1 s XPS shows three primary binding peaks at 284.7, 286.2, and 287.9 eV (Fig. 1i), associated with C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N, and C=O functionalities on the N-CQDs/AuNPs surface [45,46]. The deconvolution of the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1j) reveals two binding peaks at 531.7 and 532.7 eV, which are attributed to O=C and C-OH/C-O-C groups [47,48]. Notably, the presence of oxygen significantly impacts the emission properties of N-CQDs by balancing the sp2 and sp3 carbon configurations [42,49]. Additionally, Fig. 1k exhibits the high resolution Au 4f spectrum which displays two distinct peaks at binding energies of 83.7 and 87.4 eV, associated with the spin–orbit splitting properties of Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2, respectively, verifying the Au state in the N-CQDs/AuNPs composite [34,50]. Collectively, these findings validate the successful synthesis and functionalization of N-CQDs/AuNPs.

The freshly synthesized N-CQDs (at pH 3.4) exhibited a ζ of −7.47 mV. After the addition of AuNPs, the ζ became more negative, measuring −17 mV at pH 2 and −31 mV at pH 7. This increase in negativity indicates enhanced stability of the solution due to stronger electrostatic repulsion between particles. The negative charge of N-CQDs arises from the deprotonation of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon cores, a process that becomes more pronounced as the pH increases.

The photostability of N-CQDs/AuNPs plays a crucial role in their practical applications, and can be evaluated via fluorometry. The samples were stored in air for 60 days (Fig. S1) or exposure to continuous UV irradiation at 365 nm for 120 min (Fig. S2). Notably, the results revealed no significant reduction in fluorescence intensity after 6 weeks of storage, and also remained stable even under UV irradiation, underscoring the excellent photostability of N-CQDs/AuNPs material.

Upon adding HAuCl4 salt to the N-CQDs solution, AuNPs are formed by reduction of Au (III) to Au (0) by N-CQDs. This formation is visually confirmed by the solution's red color and the characteristic SPR absorption peak of AuNPs at 520 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum.

The partial overlap between the absorption spectrum of AuNPs and the emission spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. S3) enables significant fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). FRET describes a nonradiative energy transfer process between two chromophores: a donor chromophore in its electronically excited state transfers energy to an acceptor chromophore through dipole–dipole coupling. In this system, N-CQDs serve as the donor chromophore, transferring their excited-state energy to AuNPs, which act as the acceptor. This interaction results in fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs, a phenomenon that forms the basis for their analytical application [34,36,51].

A key characteristic of FRET is its extreme sensitivity to the distance between the donor and acceptor chromophores, as the energy transfer efficiency is inversely proportional to the sixth power of the distance between them [52]. In the N-CQDs/AuNPs system, when meropenem is introduced, its high affinity for the AuNP surface via the thioether and amine groups leads to strong interactions with AuNPs. This interaction disrupts the N-CQDs/AuNPs combination, effectively releasing the N-CQDs from proximity to the AuNPs. As the distance between N-CQDs and AuNPs increases, FRET efficiency decreases, resulting in partial recovery of the fluorescence of the solution. The extent of fluorescence recovery correlates directly with the concentration of meropenem: higher concentrations of meropenem inhibit FRET more effectively, yielding greater fluorescence recovery. The fluorescence recovery (F – F0) at 445 nm is used to quantify meropenem. This relationship establishes a reliable analytical basis for meropenem detection using fluorescence.

The distinctive color of AuNPs solutions arises from the LSPR phenomenon. LSPR occurs when surface plasmons are confined within nanoparticles of sizes comparable to or smaller than the wavelength of incident light, leading to collective oscillations of conduction electrons [53]. The oscillation frequency depends on factors such as the electron density, particle size, and shape of the charge distribution. For noble metal nanoparticles, this resonance typically occurs at visible wavelengths [54]. The LSPR wavelength is influenced by the size and interparticle spacing of the nanoparticles. For instance, as the size of a nanoparticle increases, the plasmon resonance peak shifts to longer wavelengths (lower energy), resulting in a red shift in the observed color. This shift occurs because larger nanoparticles have a reduced free electron density and a lower surface-to-volume ratio, weakening the resonance and increasing absorption at longer wavelengths [55].

In the N-CQDs/AuNPs solution, AuNPs, stabilized by surrounding N-CQDs, have sizes of approximately 20–30 nm. According to Mie theory for spherical nanoparticles, these particles exhibit a resonance peak around 520–530 nm, giving the solution its characteristic red wine color [56]. However, when meropenem adsorbs onto the AuNPs, it competes with N-CQDs for binding sites. This competitive adsorption releases the N-CQDs from the N-CQDs/AuNPs complex, leaving the AuNPs without stabilization. Consequently, the AuNPs aggregate, forming larger clusters (Fig. S4). As the size of the gold nanoparticles increases, the plasmon resonance peak shifts to longer wavelengths, around 660 nm in this case, which corresponds to a blue-colored solution (Fig. 2 ). The ratio of absorbance at 660 nm to 530 nm (A660/A530) reflects the extent of AuNP aggregation induced by meropenem. This ratio serves as an effective measure for detecting meropenem through UV–Vis spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of N-CQDs solution in the presence of AuNPs and meropenem
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the optical response of N-CQDs when interacting with meropenem, AuNPs, and the combination of AuNPs with meropenem, fluorescence spectra were recorded for N-CQDs, N-CQDs with meropenem, N-CQDs/AuNPs, and N-CQDs/AuNPs with meropenem at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 3 a). Initially, the N-CQDs solution exhibited maximum fluorescence at a wavelength of 445 nm, which is slightly red-shifted compared to undoped CQDs (typically around 440 nm). This wavelength shift is attributed to band gap modification caused by the presence of functional amine and amide groups on the surface of the N-CQDs [45]. These groups alter the electronic structure, leading to the observed red shift in the fluorescence emission.

When meropenem is added at a concentration of 100 nM, the fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs shows negligible change, indicating no direct interaction between N-CQDs and meropenem. However, in the presence of AuNPs, the fluorescence of N-CQDs is significantly quenched, resulting in an “off” state. Upon the subsequent addition of meropenem, the fluorescence is notably restored, leading to an “on” state. This fluorescence recovery, defined as the difference in fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/AuNPs in the presence and absence of meropene (F-F0), provides a sensitive basis for detecting meropenem, with detection capabilities extending to nanomolar (nM) concentrations (Fig. 3b).

The addition of meropenem induces a noticeable color change in the solution from red to blue, making the absorbance spectrum a viable analytical signal for detecting meropenem (Fig. 3c). In the UV–Vis spectrum of the N-CQDs/AuNPs/meropenem system, alongside the absorbance peak at 530 nm corresponding to the SPR wavelength of 20–30 nm AuNPs, an additional peak appears around 660 nm, representing the SPR wavelength of larger-sized AuNPs formed due to particle aggregation [57,58]. As the concentration of meropenem increases, the absorbance at 530 nm decreases, while the absorbance at 660 nm increases. The ratio of absorbance at 660 nm to 530 nm (A660/A530) serves as an effective signal for detecting meropenem using UV–Vis spectroscopy.

The procedure for quantifying meropenem using the N-CQDs/AuNPs system was optimized by evaluating several factors, including solution pH, incubation time (i.e., the time required for meropenem to interact with AuNPs), and the influence of electrolyte salt solutions. Both colorimetric and fluorescence-based sensing rely on the interaction between meropenem and AuNPs. However, since fluorescence sensing offers higher sensitivity, the measurement conditions were primarily optimized based on the fluorescence recovery signal.

For analytes having pH dependent charge, pH is a factor that can significantly affect the sensitivity of the analytical method. Within the pH range of 2 to 8, the fluorescence recovery signal showed a significant pH dependence (Fig. 4 a). At pH 2, it reached a maximum, indicating optimal conditions for fluorescence recovery. However, as the pH increased from 3 to 8, the fluorescence recovery value gradually decreased, reflecting reduced sensitivity under these conditions. At pH values below 2, the solution color changed even in the absence of meropenem, likely due to a reduction in the absolute value of the ζ at very low pH, which led to agglomeration of the N-CQDs/AuNPs.

Meropenem has 2 pKa including pKa1 ∼ 2.9 (carboxylic acid) and pKa2 ∼ 7.4 (pyrrolidinylamino) [59]. At pH < 2.9, meropenem carries positive charge on the pyrolidinylamino group. Between pH 2.9 and 7.4, as the carboxylic acid deprotonates, a portion of the meropenem molecules become negatively charged, while the positive charge decreases. At pH values greater than 7.4, the pyrrolidinylamino group of meropenem becomes deprotonated, making the molecule fully negatively charged (Fig. 4c). For N-CQDs/AuNPs, the surface charge is negative even at low pH and becomes more negative as the pH increases. Therefore, one can expect a decreasing attractive force between N-CQDs/AuNPs and meropenem as the pH increases. As can be seen from ζ measurement (Fig. 4b), with adsorption of meropenem on N-CQDs/AuNPs at pH 2, the ζ increased from −17 mV to −4 mV; meanwhile the ζ of N-CQDs/AuNPs hardly changed at pH 7, from −31.9 mV to −30 mV.

Sufficient time is essential for meropenem to bind to AuNPs and release free N-CQDs, making incubation time a critical factor affecting fluorescence recovery. As shown in Fig. 4d, the N-CQDs/AuNPs system exhibited a rapid response to meropenem, stabilizing within 10 min. Visually, the solution's color changed from red to blue (indicating AuNPs clustering) immediately after meropenem addition and stabilized after 10 min. Therefore, 10 min was selected as the optimal incubation time for subsequent analyses.

Electrolyte salts have the effect of shrinking the electric double layer on the surface of AuNPs, thereby enhancing electrostatic interactions between AuNPs and meropenem molecules. Influence of typical electrolyte salts such as NaCl, KNO3, CuSO4, MgCl2, Na2SO4, with concentrations varying from 0 to 0.16 mM, on fluorescence recovery is presented in Fig. 4e. Among these salts, doubly charged ions like Mg2+ and SO4 2− showed a more pronounced effect on fluorescence recovery compared to singly charged ions such as Na+, K+ and Cl−. This correlation can be attributed to the higher valence of divalent ions, which reduces the ζ more effectively and enhances electrostatic interactions, promoting stronger adsorption of meropenem on the negatively N-CQDs/AuNPs surface [37,60].

An especially notable effect was observed for CuSO4, which produced significantly higher fluorescence recovery than other salts. This enhancement aligns with our previous research on cysteine detection using AuNPs, where Cu2+ exhibited complexation behavior with –NH2 groups in cysteine [31]. In this case, similarly, Cu2+ can be assumed to complex with –NH2 groups in meropenem and/or –NH2 groups on the N-CQDs surface. Thus, Cu2+ can act as a cross-linking agent for pairs of meropenem-coated AuNPs, thereby enhancing the separation of AuNPs from N-CQDs, effectively increasing the fluorescence recovery signal [61]. Additionally, increasing the salt concentration generally led to an increase in fluorescence recovery. However, at concentrations exceeding 0.1 mM, the solution began to change color even in the absence of meropenem, likely due to excessive destabilization of the N-CQDs/AuNPs system. Based on these findings, CuSO4 at a concentration of 0.1 mM was selected as the optimal electrolyte salt for further investigations under the given conditions.

The quantification of meropenem using N-CQDs/AuNPs with and without nitrogen doping were validated with criteria of linear range, calibration curve, limit of detection (LOD), and precision as measured by relative standard deviation (RSD %) (Table 1 ). For the colorimetric method, the absorbance ratio A660/A530 was found to be linear with the meropenem concentration in the wide range from 80 − 850 nM.

The combination of N-CQDs and AuNPs can be used as a sensitive dual sensor for meropenem detection. Whereas fluorescence sensing has a lower detection limit (LOD = 2.5 nM = 1 ppb) than colorimetric sensing (LOD = 25 nM = 10 ppb), the colorimetric method provides naked eye visual detection, suggesting development of a simple paper-based test for quick screening (Fig. 5 a-b). Comparing the figures of merit between meropenem quantification using N-CQDs/AuNPs and CQDs/AuNPs, it can be seen that doping nitrogen into CQDs improves the sensitivity of quantification, possibly due to the greater abundance of –NH2 functional groups on CQDs surface, that can interact better with AuNPs, giving higher fluorescence quantum yield.

LOD of the fluorescence method is lower than most methods and comparable to SWV method, proving that the method has good sensitivity and high analysis efficiency. It should be noted that this method provides much lower LOD than the CE-C4D method (0.7 ppm) and colorimetric method using citrate-capped AuNPs (0.64 ppm), and mostly comparable with the cross-check LC-MS/MS (3 ppb) approach (Table 2 ). Hence, fluorescence can be applied not only for pharmaceutical samples but also to measure traces of meropenem in patient’s plasma at different times after drug injection.

The selectivity of the method was evaluated using five β-lactam antibiotics: meropenem, ertapenem, penicillin V, cefalexin, and amoxicillin with the same concentration of 1 µM. Based on visual observation of color changes, meropenem was easily distinguishable from penicillin V, cefalexin, and amoxicillin but not from ertapenem, which belongs to the same carbapenem class. Regarding fluorescence recovery, antibiotics outside the carbapenem group showed minimal impact on the signal, even at concentrations 10 times higher than meropenem (Fig. 5c). Furthermore, potential coexisting substances in pharmaceutical preparations, such as glucose, sucrose, lactose, Na2CO3, Zn2+ and Mg2+, had negligible interference with the detection of meropenem.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in meropenem determination of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Meropenem in pharmaceutical and human plasma samples was detected through the color change of N-CQDs/AuNPs upon adding sample and quantified using fluorescence. When human plasma samples were added, the red wine color of N-CQDs/AuNPs turned to purple, whereas the addition of pharmaceutical samples results in a lighter purple color. For pharmaceuticals, the difference between the meropenem content analyzed by the fluorescence using N-CQDs/AuNPs and the label content are in the range of 1.2–3.3 %. Cross-checking with LC-MS/MS gives a small deviation of 1.8 and 2.1 % for Meropenem kabi and Medozopen (VN-16323–12) samples, respectively (Table 3 ).

The applicability of the developed method was validated using blank human plasma samples spiked with 1.25 and 5.00 µM meropenem. The method demonstrated high recovery efficiency (83.2 – 87.0 %) with a relative standard deviation of less than 5 % (Table 4 ). Comparison with cross-check results from the LC-MS/MS method revealed an insignificant difference of 5.58 %, confirming that the developed method provides reliable and accurate results.

In conclusion, the combination of N-CQDs and gold nanoparticles enables reliable detection and quantification of meropenem across various sample matrices, ranging from simple pharmaceutical formulations to complex human plasma, with a LOD comparable to standard LC-MS/MS methods. However, the method's primary limitation is its low selectivity for distinguishing meropenem from other antibiotics in the carbapenem group due to their structural similarities and the inability to quantify them simultaneously.

N-CQDs synthesized using a simple, rapid method in a domestic microwave oven instead of conventional pyrolysis have demonstrated significant potential for antibiotic analysis. The combination of N-CQDs and AuNPs provides a low-cost, selective, sensitive, and precise dual-sensor platform for meropenem detection and quantification in various samples. N-CQDs serve three critical roles in this system: as a reducing agent, a stabilizing agent, and a fluorescence energy donor for AuNPs. The colorimetric sensing mode allows for visual detection of meropenem, while the fluorometric sensing mode offers higher sensitivity, making it suitable for quantifying meropenem at low concentrations in real samples. Validation via LC-MS/MS confirmed the method's reliability for analyzing pharmaceutical and human plasma samples. This approach has the potential to be expanded for analyzing other compounds that interact with AuNPs but do not directly respond to CQDs.
